title: ODI Matrix: Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools

sections.title: Formula
sections.content:
```
opportunity_score = importance + max(importance - satisfaction, 0)
is_underserved    = importance > 7.0 AND satisfaction < 5.0
is_overserved     = importance < 3.0 AND satisfaction > 7.0
```

---

sections.content:

---

sections.title: Step 2 — Satisfaction Recalibration (Incumbent Evidence)
sections.content:
| Incumbent | Est. Campus Share | Mechanism | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A3: Basic prop alarms** (Detex EC-100, Seco-Larm) | ~64% | Contact/spring alarm strip | Low cost, simple install, mechanical reliability, no power infrastructure | Local alarm only — no centralized monitoring, no compliance logging, no battery visibility |
| **A5: Patrol + manual log** | ~15% | Human rounds + clipboard | Zero hardware cost | No real-time detection, patrol gaps overnight, manual documentation error-prone |
| **A2: Electronic exit control** (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic) | ~10–15% | Mains-powered electronic control with central software | BMS integration, centralized monitoring, structured logging | Requires mains wiring — incompatible with >75% legacy campus buildings; 3–5× cost |
| **A4: Access control + alarmed hardware** (Allegion Von Duprin, ASSA ABLOY Aperio) | ~6% | Credential + alarmed release | Integrated credential management | Complex IT provisioning, wired, designed for access control not alarm monitoring |

**Recalibration verdict:** Original Step 06 satisfaction scores are consistent with incumbent profile. A3 dominates at 64% and provides zero monitoring, documentation, or wireless capability — scores of 3–4 for monitoring/compliance needs are appropriate and are kept unchanged. No satisfaction score adjusted.

---

U/S = is_underserved | O/S = is_overserved | PR = product_related

**N08 note:** importance=9 but satisfaction=5 (not <5) → is_underserved=false. Incumbents meet the mechanical reliability need adequately via basic prop alarms.

---

sections.title: Step 4 — Market ODI Summary
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Market** | Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools |
| **NAICS** | 611310 |
| **Total needs** | 28 |
| **Underserved count** | 14 |
| **Overserved count** | 0 |
| **Avg opportunity score** | 11.68 |
| **Zone** | Appropriately Served / low-Underserved boundary |

**Top 5 opportunities:**
1. (15) Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
2. (15) Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
3. (14) Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
4. (14) Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
5. (13) Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range

---

sections.title: Step 5 — Cross-Check: ODI vs Feature-Fit (Step 09)
sections.content:
| Metric | Value | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| ODI avg opportunity score | 11.68 | >12 → "high opportunity, low fit" flag trigger |
| Feature-Fit avg (Step 09) | 6.24 (mean of 8 features, 5.00–7.78) | <4 → flag trigger |

**Result: No inconsistency.** ODI avg (11.68) does not exceed 12; Feature-Fit avg (6.24) does not fall below 4. Both metrics are consistent: moderately high unmet need with adequate product-to-market feature alignment. The market has strong underserved clusters (compliance documentation, real-time monitoring) where product features (F8 Automated Event Log, F6 Battery Status Reporting) provide direct coverage.

---

rows.Strengths: Low cost, simple install, mechanical reliability, no power infrastructure
rows.Weaknesses: Local alarm only — no centralized monitoring, no compliance logging, no battery visibility
No real-time detection, patrol gaps overnight, manual documentation error-prone

rows.Incumbent: **A2: Electronic exit control** (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic)
rows.Mechanism: Mains-powered electronic control with central software
rows.Strengths: BMS integration, centralized monitoring, structured logging
rows.Weaknesses: Requires mains wiring — incompatible with >75% legacy campus buildings; 3–5× cost

rows.Incumbent: **A4: Access control + alarmed hardware** (Allegion Von Duprin, ASSA ABLOY Aperio)
rows.Weaknesses: Complex IT provisioning, wired, designed for access control not alarm monitoring

entities.product: GfS Türwächter IoT (Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT)

top_opportunities: Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range

entries.error_statement: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
Minimize the effort required to assess door hardware compatibility and wireless signal reach across heterogeneous campus building stock before device selection
Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range
Minimize installation labor-hours required to deploy exit monitoring devices across a multi-building campus without licensed electrical contractor involvement
Minimize variability in alarm guard installation quality across lever-handle, panic-bar, and glass-frame door configurations during a campus-wide rollout
Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit alarm devices with undetected wireless connectivity failures that are not discovered until after campus go-live
Minimize the effort required to verify wireless transmission integrity between each newly installed device and the central receiver before signing off on commissioning
Minimize the likelihood of alarm guard mechanical failure in high-traffic campus exit environments (dormitories, student unions) causing an unguarded door state
Minimize the likelihood of false alarm triggers caused by accidental door contact disrupting normal building operations
Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
Minimize the number of door events failing to reach the central monitoring dashboard due to RF signal attenuation in reinforced-concrete campus buildings
Minimize variability in battery depletion rates across devices deployed in buildings with different door-use frequencies and ambient temperatures
Minimize the time between a campus emergency exit device losing wireless connectivity and the security operations center detecting the monitoring gap
Increase the ability to monitor all campus emergency exit devices from a single security dashboard without requiring building-specific logins or separate hardware systems
Minimize the time required to replace an exit alarm guard battery without triggering the alarm horn or temporarily disabling the door's fire-code escape path
Minimize the time to restore full exit alarm and wireless monitoring function after a device fault or battery exhaustion event
Minimize the labor required to compile emergency exit compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report filings
Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
Increase the ability to scale emergency exit monitoring incrementally across a campus building-by-building without system-wide infrastructure changes or full re-commissioning
Minimize the effort required to evaluate exit monitoring vendor interoperability with existing campus PSIM and Building Management Systems before procurement commitment
Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act Annual Security Report inaccuracies caused by manual compilation of door-event records that were not captured in a central digital log

Minimize the likelihood of wireless signal attenuation below reliable reporting threshold in reinforced-concrete campus buildings where structural floors and walls exceed the 30 m EasyWave integrated transmitter range

Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered only after campus-wide deployment, requiring unplanned repeater infrastructure additions that increase per-door cost and delay full-campus monitoring go-live

Minimize the likelihood of IoT battery exhaustion on dormitory emergency exits where 24/7 student occupancy and high door-event frequency depletes a 9V block battery before the scheduled 2-year maintenance cycle

Minimize the integration effort required to relay EasyWave alarm events into campus PSIM or building automation platforms standardized on BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols via GfS-proprietary BUS gateway

Minimize the likelihood of door-event timestamps logged by the IoT monitoring system creating FERPA or EU GDPR compliance exposure when access event records are attributable to identifiable resident students

Minimize the likelihood of surface-mounted exit alarm guard housings being physically tampered with or dislodged in student dormitory corridors, leaving an emergency exit in an unguarded state without a monitoring system alert
